data.title: Global X Silver Miners ETF: Leveraged Exposure To Silver As The Cycle Extends Into 2026
data.description:
Currently silver occupies a central place in the market, since, in recent months, the metal has been reaching historical highs, accompanied by gold also breaking records. And I do not consider it to be a one-off movement in prices, but how the market sees the macro forward.

What does the market see today? As the following SA article explains very well, lower rates are expected, a less dominant dollar and a still tense geopolitical context:

Traders are betting the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates twice in 2026 after a host of economic data last week, while U.S. President Donald Trump has also advocated for looser monetary policy.

Elsewhere, rising geopolitical tensions in recent weeks have further strengthened the safe-haven appeal of gold and silver. The United States has tightened its oil blockade on Venezuela, increasing pressure on President Nicolas Maduro’s government, while Ukraine has, for the first time, attacked an oil tanker belonging to Russia’s shadow fleet in the Mediterranean Sea.

An extra point to highlight is that silver usually behaves differently from gold. It maintains its role as a safe-haven asset, also facing a relevant industrial demand, which explains why, when the cycle is activated, silver tends to move more intensively.

When that happens, the miners tend to amplify the movement. With largely fixed costs, metal price changes directly impact margins and cash generation, which is where Global X Silver Miners ETF (SIL) comes in as a simple way to capture that operational leverage without having to select a single company.

What kind of exposure SIL offers?

SIL is a very direct ETF, since almost all the fund is invested in companies in the basic materials sector, mainly mining linked to silver. The level of cash is minimal and there are no defensive assets to cushion the cycles.

Although the ETF has more than 40 positions, the portfolio is quite concentrated: the top ten account for about 75% of the total, which in practice means that the fund’s performance depends largely on a few companies. There are primary silver producers, diversified companies and some royalty firms that bring some more relative stability, as we see in the table below, but the profile is still clearly cyclical.

This structure explains why the ETF tends to move more than the metal itself, both up and down.

The recent rally and what the numbers show

When I look at the momentum, I see that the recent movement of the ETF was strong. In the last month, SIL rose about 25%, and in the last 6 months, the advance exceeds 70%. In year-to-year terms, the rise is around 160%, well above the S&P 500 in the same period.

In the longer term, the pattern is maintained, although with an irregular path. In 3 years, the ETF accumulates increases close to 220%. It is not a background of smooth trajectories, but of marked cycles and high volatility.

Beyond price, some data helps put the rally in context. The companies that integrate the ETF show an ROE close to 12%, with an average capitalization of almost $19B. At the same time, multiples are being adjusted as results improve, with the P/E ratio falling from very high levels to more reasonable values.

This does not eliminate the risk, but suggests that some of the recent advance is also accompanied by improvements in fundamentals and not just euphoria.

How much can SIL be worth in 2026?

Thinking about 2026, I estimated three scenarios, using as a reference the price of silver and the historical behavior of mining companies, rather than external target prices.

If silver corrected to lower levels, the impact on mining would be rapid. On the other hand, I believe that if it manages to consolidate at high levels for longer, the effect on margins and cash generation may be more durable and sustainable.

From that approach, I got these price ranges for SIL in 2026, based on a current price close to $88.

In a more conservative scenario, with silver down to the $55-60/oz zone, the impact on mining would be rapid. Given the operational leverage of the sector, a drop in the metal is usually amplified in earnings and stock price, which could lead SIL to move in an approximate range of $70-80.

My base scenario for 2026 assumes that silver consolidates between $65 and $70 per ounce. In that context, I do not expect a new explosive rally, but a more gradual extension of the cycle, as I estimate that with sustained metal prices and still healthy margins, SIL could place itself in a $90-100 range.

In a more bullish scenario, with silver surpassing $75 per ounce, I believe that operational leverage would play back in mining’s favor. If that level is maintained over a prolonged period, the ETF would have room to look for higher levels, in the $110-120 area, although with high volatility along the way.

Risks

The main risk I observe is a correction in the price of silver. Given the operational leverage of mining companies, any relevant metal decline is usually amplified in the ETF.

Operational and political risks in some mining jurisdictions must also be considered, as well as the portfolio concentration itself.

Moreover, after such a strong rally, periods of consolidation or corrections cannot be ruled out, even in the current context.

Conclusion

From my point of view, SIL remains an efficient tool to expose yourself to the silver cycle through mining, in a context where metal is kept at historically high levels and the macro environment remains supportive. The ETF does exactly what it promises: amplifies metal movements.

At the same time, much of the initial movement has already occurred, and from these levels the return risk profile is positive, but more balanced. For investors who understand the volatility of the sector and maintain a constructive view on silver towards 2026, my stance is Buy, as I consider that SIL continues to make sense within a diversified portfolio.

data.title: Aeluma: When A Semiconductor Story Stops Being A Science Project
data.description:
A year ago this was a science project. Today, it looks like a company preparing to execute.

Author's Note:

During much of 2025, I have conducted a deep, ongoing diligence process on Aeluma (ALMU) to understand not only its technology but also its evolving commercial posture. My interest stems from how rarely investors encounter a public semiconductor company at this stage: one built on years of non-dilutive government funding, validated technology, and a growing roster of sophisticated customers — yet still early enough that the market has not fully adjusted its expectations.

Aeluma operates at the intersection of compound semiconductors, photonics, and advanced manufacturing, with potential applications spanning aerospace and defense, AI infrastructure, quantum, consumer devices, and wearables. As I noted in my earlier 2025 articles (Prati - ALMU article 1) and (Prati - ALMU article 2), the technical foundation has long been compelling. What has changed more recently is the company’s posture: Aeluma is transitioning from a research-driven organization into an execution-focused commercial enterprise.

Founder and CEO Jonathan Klamkin brings deep technical credibility and is now demonstrating increasing operational leadership as the company moves toward design wins and early commercial revenue. For investors willing to do the work, Aeluma represents a rare opportunity to study — and potentially own — a company at the moment when technical risk begins to give way to execution risk. These moments are often understated in headlines, but they are where long-term value creation typically begins. The Aeluma story has quietly changed from "interesting" to "inevitable."

Why Tone Matters More Than Headlines

In early 2025, Aeluma was widely perceived as an R&D-heavy photonics developer — technologically impressive, but commercially uncertain.

That perception was not irrational. Qualification timelines were long, customer programs were still exploratory, and management communication leaned deliberately conservative. Investors were asked, repeatedly, to be patient.

What has changed over the past nine months is not a single announcement or headline-grabbing press release. It is the consistency and direction of management tone across multiple touchpoints, combined with observable shifts in customer behavior. In semiconductors, these qualitative signals often precede financial inflection by several quarters. Markets are generally poor at pricing such transitions — even though they often mark the most attractive entry points.

At the center of this shift is a manufacturing platform that quietly moved Aeluma from technical validation toward commercial relevance.

September 2025: Maximum Conservatism

The FY2026 outlook provided in early September was notably restrained.

Management emphasized:

  • Long qualification timelines
  • Customer diligence and testing cycles
  • The distinction between engagement and commercialization

This posture was appropriate — and arguably necessary — given Aeluma’s history and the broader skepticism surrounding the commercialization of photonics.

In hindsight, however, that call marked the low point in expressed confidence. Not because fundamentals deteriorated, but because the company was transitioning from proof to preparation.

November 12: A Subtle but Meaningful Pivot

By the Q1 earnings call in November, the language had changed.

Not dramatically — but unmistakably.

Management began emphasizing:

  • The number of active customer engagements
  • The maturity of several programs
  • Discussions shifting toward yield, reliability, and manufacturability

Importantly, the conversation moved away from “Does the technology work?” toward “How does this integrate into our roadmap?”

That distinction matters enormously. In semiconductors, integration planning is the precursor to design wins, even if formal announcements lag.

December Fireside Chat: “Go Time” Without the Hype

The Benchmark fireside chat from last Thursday represented a third — and decisive — tonal evolution.

What stood out was not promotional language but operational specificity:

  • Execution cadence replaced long-term vision
  • Customer discussions were framed around timelines and scale
  • Internal focus shifted toward hiring, manufacturing readiness, and throughput

The phrase “it’s go time” was notable not because it was enthusiastic, but because it was matter-of-fact. It reflected urgency rooted in customer pull rather than aspirational optimism.

In academic terms, this looked clearly like a company crossing from technology risk into execution risk — a far more attractive position for long-term capital.

Why This Transition Is Easy to Miss

Aeluma does not yet have:

  • Named hyperscaler design wins
  • Large, visible commercial revenue
  • Clean quarterly inflection points

As a result, the stock continues to trade as though little has changed.

But internally, the company now appears to be operating in a different phase:

  • Customers are allocating engineering resources
  • Manufacturing discussions are active
  • Late-stage engagements are narrowing toward qualification

These are the conditions under which semiconductor businesses quietly re-rate — often before revenue becomes obvious.

The Valuation Disconnect

Even conservative external models imply a materially different company by 2028.

Yet today’s valuation still reflects:

  • Skepticism about commercialization
  • Fatigue from prior dilution
  • Discomfort with long timelines

That disconnect creates an unusual setup: perceived risk is declining faster than its valuation reflects.

For investors with a multi-year horizon, this is precisely where asymmetry tends to emerge.

Why This Is Increasingly a “Tuck-Away” Idea

Aeluma does not need to become a household name in 2026 for the thesis to work.

It needs only to:

  • Convert late-stage engagements into qualification
  • Begin modest commercial ramps
  • Demonstrate repeatability and yield

If those conditions are met, the valuation framework changes — regardless of short-term volatility.

This is not about predicting the next quarter. It is about recognizing when a company has crossed an internal threshold that the market has not yet priced.

A Note for Retail and Institutional Investors Alike

For retail investors, opportunities to buy into companies with validated technology, strong IP, and early commercial momentum — before institutional ownership becomes inevitable — are genuinely rare.

For institutional investors, the hesitation is understandable: Aeluma is small, illiquid, and early. But that is precisely the point. Structural constraints often prevent meaningful exposure during the most asymmetric phase of value creation.

This is not a base-case forecast, but it illustrates the optionality: if Aeluma executes into even a few hundred million dollars of revenue over the coming years, the company’s scale — and market perception — would change dramatically. The largest returns in semiconductors are often captured not by waiting for certainty, but by recognizing transition early.

Final Thought

Markets are excellent at pricing certainty and terrible at pricing transition.

Over the past year, Aeluma has moved from cautious validation toward confident execution — not in headlines, but in behavior, tone, and operational focus.

For investors willing to look past near-term noise, this increasingly resembles the early stages of a multi-year re-rating — the kind best captured by buying early and doing very little afterward.

Author’s Note: This article was written entirely by the author without the use of generative AI tools. All analyses and conclusions are original and based on publicly available information.

data.title: Lowe's: Still Looking For Reasons To Get Excited
data.description:
Lowe's (LOW) shares have yielded fairly underwhelming results over the past five years, significantly lagging behind the market.

From first principles, if I had told you that EPS and revenue would grow at a mid-single-digit rate, with the stock coming into this stretch at a 25x+ P/E multiple, you would probably guess that results would be poor.

Well, I argue that the current situation isn't that much different and that investors should patiently wait for triggers.

Lowe's Past Five Years & Investing First Principles

If I had to narrow down all of my investing lessons into one rule, it would be Peter Lynch's rule that stocks follow earnings growth.

Once you truly understand this rule, the investing analysis process becomes much simpler. You realize that in order to generate market-besting returns, you must identify companies that have under-appreciated earnings growth trajectories.

Such under-appreciation can take the form of a low multiple relative to growth, or growth expectations that are too low, either near-term or long-term.

This is where Buffett's wide moat kicks in. Wide moat companies tend to generate far better growth over the long term than anyone is willing to model out today, which gives them an inherent under-appreciation.

Over the past 5 years, Lowe's stock severely lagged the market, with about 38 points of underperformance.

From the Peter Lynch rule perspective, it's easy to understand why. Despite growing mid-single-digits annually, lower than the market average, Lowe's consistently traded above market-average valuation.

This is precisely the opposite of under-appreciation. There are several explanations for the market being overly optimistic about Lowe's.

The company is historical, has a track record of strong returns, is entrenched in US retail, and it appeals to dividend investors who like its long dividend streak.

Note, neither of those reasons fits the Peter Lynch rule of yielding good returns. Now, the question is, can this happen?

Last Quarter Was More Of The Same

In the recently announced quarter, Lowe's revenue was $20.8 billion, up 3% Y/Y, mainly driven by comps sales of 0.4%, and online sales growth of 11.4%. Dissecting comps, transactions were down 3.0%, while the average ticket was up 3.4%, not the best mix. In addition, comps trended worse and worse from month to month, in line with comps.

EPS was down 3.7% Y/Y, as a 50bps improvement in gross margin was more than offset by higher operating expenses, leading to a 65bps contraction in operating margin. On an adjusted basis, operating margins were up 10 bps, and adjusted EPS was up 5.9% Y/Y.

The adjustments include costs associated with Lowe's acquisitions of Foundation Building Materials and Artisan Design Group, which are aimed at enhancing its offerings, especially for professional customers.

All up, Lowe's once again missed expectations, this time for both revenue and GAAP EPS, making it the second quarter of 2025 with a miss. This takes me back to my over-appreciation comment. As we can see, this isn't the case with Lowe's.

For their near-term outlook, Lowe's reaffirmed or downgraded certain guidance items, which now include the FBM contribution impact.

Comparable sales are now expected to be flat, vs. flat to up 1% previously. Adjusted operating margins are expected to be lower, at 12.1%, and adjusted EPS is expected to be at the low-end of the previous range.

I don't think you can look at the quarter's numbers and sense a beginning of a reignition or find a signal that we're on the verge of acceleration. To me, this is pretty much more of the same.

Lowe's Valuation - Craziness Is Doing The Same Thing And Expecting Different Results

At $240 per share, Lowe's trades at 20 times forward earnings and 19 times next year's estimates.

While this valuation finally places Lowe's below the market average multiple of 22x, this is still quite relative to Lowe's growth prospects.

Consensus sees Lowe's EPS growth accelerating to 8% by 2027. If we take that as the normalized growth rate, the current P/E multiple would take us to a PEG ratio of 2.5x, which is considered very high.

This is a similar situation to the one five years ago, and I don’t see any reason to think it will yield a different result. To me, a 2x PEG, or 16 times earnings, is where Lowe's ultimately becomes interesting.

Potential Triggers

Up until now, we talked about why Lowe's is not an interesting investment, but that's easy. Let's try to identify what could occur that will change that.

I think that by far, the best thing that could happen for Lowe's is a sudden boom in home improvements and home building, two markets that don't always overlap, but sometimes they do.

Right now, whether it's macro, interest rates, tariffs, deportations, or still dealing with the COVID pull-forward, none of those markets are exactly booming.

Existing home sales (a key leading indicator for home improvement) are still around 10-year lows, while housing starts are at 5-year lows.

Until mortgage rates fall low enough so that both these industries reignite, I don't see a path for meaningful acceleration for Lowe's. Even as Lowe's tries to lean more heavily on professionals, it is still sensitive to the same end markets.

In other words, its professional-forward strategy doesn't change the fact that the number one data point I would monitor before entering a position is mortgage rates, as it solves both end markets.

Lower rates allow for more existing home sales, which then allow more new home sales, which then drive more housing starts, and that's the magic flywheel that needs to get going.

Conclusion

Lowe's is a legendary American company that probably won't be disrupted in this lifetime. As safe as it is, equity investors should look for upside, not safety.

I still don't see a justification to buy the stock at this PEG, as the path for market-beating acceleration remains uncertain.

As such, I reiterate a Hold.

data.title: How A Potential 2026 Market Rotation Has Completely Reshaped My Portfolio
data.description:
Over the past several years, the S&P 500 (SPY) soared higher largely due to outperformance in the technology sector (QQQ). While there has been strong earnings growth among many tech stocks, much of the total returns have also been driven by valuation-multiple expansion among tech stocks, as bullish expectations for future AI-driven profitability have caused investors to bid up the valuation multiples for leading companies in the AI space.

As the chart below illustrates, this has led to the S&P 500’s price-earnings multiple to expand significantly to where it's now in strongly overvalued territory.

Given that the market has reached such a stretched valuation due to tech stocks, I expect that in 2026, market leadership is likely going to rotate away from tech (XLK) toward cash flow generating and attractively valued energy (XLE). This article will detail why.

The Macro Backdrop Few Investors Are Taking Seriously

Before I get into the specifics of these two sectors, I'm first going to provide a bit of a macro backdrop. Right now, the U.S. economy is being plagued by persistent inflation. While the most recent inflation reading was better than many feared, it was still well above the Fed’s 2% target level. Additionally, there's rising unemployment in the economy, and the consumer and government are heavily burdened with debt. Not only that, but there are huge structural fiscal deficits for the U.S. government that are causing its debt to grow faster than the economy is.

However, while there are these aforementioned concerning signs that could cause a major market correction, which would likely hit tech as hard as any sector given its bloated valuations, even if the economy remains on sound footing in 2026, tech still could very possibly lag the broader market, and energy in particular. This is because the tech sector’s advances have largely been driven by valuation multiple expansion, which is simply a reflection of market sentiment, and market sentiment by definition can change, even if underlying earnings do not.

Why Rate Cuts Don’t Automatically Save Tech

Additionally, while the Fed has been cutting rates in recent months and is expected to cut rates at least once in 2026, rate cuts do not automatically mean that tech is moving higher in 2026. This is because tech is more sensitive to long-term interest rates than short-term interest rates, since much of its cash flows are long-dated into the future, as they're mostly speculative cash flows that come from expected profits due to AI advancement and adoption. Meanwhile, rate cuts by the Fed have a much more direct impact on short-term interest rates than long-term interest rates since long-term interest rates are also subject to disruptions in the bond market, which include heavy Treasury (TLT) issuance to cover fiscal deficits and potential dumping of Treasuries by major foreign holders like China.

Energy’s Cash Flow Advantage in a Changing Market

Meanwhile, energy, with its status as a major inflation hedge and its high degree of cash flows, much of which are paid out as dividends (as illustrated by the likes of Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX), which have attractive dividend yields, and in particular in the midstream infrastructure space (AMLP) with the likes of Enbridge (ENB) and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) offering even higher yields), is less sensitive to long-duration cash flow analysis and is more of a near-term earned-profits-today business model.

The contrast between the attractive valuations for energy and tech’s stretched valuations is even more pronounced when taking into account the fact that the energy sector’s balance sheets are as strong as ever due to strong capital discipline in recent years and an emphasis on paying down debt while also increasing shareholder returns through buybacks and dividends. Meanwhile, tech is placing increasingly risky and aggressive bets on AI-related capex through massive data center and other infrastructure build-outs. Not only is this investment vast in scale and requiring some companies like Oracle (ORCL) and Meta (META) to take on a large amount of debt, but the return on investment, and in particular the timeline of the return on the investment, is still very unclear, given that there is still so much uncertainty about how much AI will transform society and how rapidly it will achieve that transformation.

This uncertainty, coinciding with increasingly stretched valuations in the AI sector, makes energy a likely beneficiary when the market rotates away from bullish yet speculative bets on future technological innovation and instead begins to place greater value on profitability and cash flow.

AI’s Dependence on Energy Infrastructure

Not only that, but energy is also likely to benefit from a future in which AI plays a massive role in the economy. This is because AI is largely dependent on energy because data centers, or as NVIDIA's (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang likes to label them, "AI factories," receive data as an input, along with immense amounts of energy, in order to output intelligence. Thus, they require a stable, affordable, and preferably clean source of power, which makes natural gas an ideal source of power, especially in the United States, where it's abundant and relatively inexpensive. This makes midstream infrastructure businesses, as well as energy production companies that have substantial exposure to natural gas, likely to be big beneficiaries of the AI boom.

Additionally, while the tech AI plays still bear a lot of execution risk and monetization risk, the energy and energy infrastructure companies that own natural gas assets, especially ones that are well-located relative to AI data centers, stand to benefit regardless of exactly how profitable AI investments turn out to be or which AI companies turn out to be the biggest winners. Meanwhile, if we do fall into a stagflationary environment, energy midstream should hold up quite well because its assets are largely fee-based with take-or-pay contracts, and they have inflation-linked escalators as well as low commodity price sensitivity and strong investment-grade balance sheets. Moreover, they should still be growing EBITDA due to significant projects coming online, including tailwinds from the AI build-out.

Meanwhile, energy production companies may suffer more cash flow volatility in the near term, but over the long term, they too should benefit from AI and re-industrialization in the United States along with a growing energy export boom from the U.S. Given their attractive valuations today, even short-term headwinds from weak energy pricing and a potential economic downturn will likely not be enough to destroy the long-term investment thesis and still make them very attractive risk-adjusted bets relative to AI-related tech stocks that have gotten priced up to nosebleed valuation levels.

Investor Takeaway

That being said, this is merely a projection, and market projections over a one-year period or less are notoriously hard to get right, so even though I'm heavily overweight the energy sector right now and underweight tech, it would not shock me at all if this projection turned out to be wrong over the next year. After all, as Benjamin Graham once said,

In the short run, the stock market is a voting machine. But in the long run, it is a weighing machine.

Nevertheless, I believe that over the long term, on a risk-adjusted basis, energy remains a much more attractive opportunity than tech is at current valuations. As a result, I remain significantly overweight energy in my portfolio while I'm largely steering clear of tech stocks at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Shoulder Innovations: Interesting, But Not Having My Backing Yet
data.description:
Shares of Shoulder Innovations (SI) have been trading around the IPO price, as I concluded that I was interested but not convinced back in August. After shares were priced below the IPO range, they traded at reasonable sales multiples, but ongoing losses, product concentration, and competition were causes for concern.

Continued revenue gains have been solid but have come with higher losses post-IPO, as anecdotal evidence suggests further advancements in 2026, although not quantified yet.

All About the Shoulder

Shoulder Innovations aims to provide better shoulder surgical care, and despite the company growing sales and trading at reasonable sales multiples, investors acted with caution around the time of the offering over the summer.

The company focuses on transforming the shoulder surgical care market, which it does by offering advanced implant systems for shoulder arthroplasty, thereby offering a complete suite of products and services. This includes preparation of planning, implant design, and overall procedure efficiency to thereby deliver superior patient outcomes.

Improvements in this field are badly needed, with shoulder pain being both chronic and prevalent, with sources including osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, fractures, and rotator cuff tears. In fact, millions of physician visits relate to shoulder issues.

The initial target market of the business was that of shoulder arthroplasty, being an established surgical procedure that involves reconstruction of the shoulder joint with prosthetic implants. This can be done through either an anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty (aTSA) or through a reverse total shoulder arthroplasty (rTSA). Such procedures can be performed in inpatient hospital settings as well as ambulatory surgery centers.

The company claims superior advantages to traditional implants, based on improved mechanical fit, improved kinematics, specialized support, etc., all designed to improve mobility, reduce pain, and reduce healthcare costs.

With products marketed for both procedure types, Shoulder Innovations has further potential here, after the first technology was FDA cleared as recently as 2021.

IPO Pricing

Shoulder Innovations aimed to go public at a midpoint of $20 per share, yet final pricing was only set at $15 per share. With nearly 20 million shares outstanding, the market value of the firm stood at $300 million, including a roughly $150 million net cash position following the offering.

This valuation was applied to a business that generated $19 million in sales in 2023, although accompanied by a near $11 million operating loss. Revenues rose some 63% to more than $31 million in 2024, as operating losses increased in a smaller manner to $15 million. Revenues were generated from over 4,300 systems being sold, with the average selling price per unit coming in at over $7,000.

Reported sales rose some 40% to $10.1 million in the first quarter of this year, as second-quarter sales growth slowed down to 32%, with revenues reported at $11.0 million. With revenues trending over $40 million each year, operating asset valuations came in at just 3-4 times sales, although this comes with large losses and slower growth.

Moreover, there are risks relating to a concentrated product suite, regulatory and competitive risks, as well as slower growth and continued losses reported.

Performing a balancing act, I concluded that while modest sales multiples looked compelling, there was a lot of work to be done. This is notably related to sales growth, improving margins, and creating more diversified operations, all of which made me rather cautious here.

Modest Advancements

Formally, the second quarter results were released in September, with revenues reported at $11.0 million, although they came with an operating loss of $5.9 million. After posting revenues at $21.1 million in the first half of the year, a full-year sales guidance calling for sales around $43 million did not look too impressive. As is often the case, management guided conservatively soon after an initial offering.

Later that month, Shoulder Innovations announced the full commercial launch of the InSet 70 Humeral Stem, expanding the product line of the 95 version, which debuted last year.

In November, Shoulder reported third-quarter sales at $11.8 million, with revenues up 58% on the year before, following a weaker third quarter last year, all driven by a 53% increase in the number of units sold. So far, this is good news; however, operating losses rose further to $7.6 million, in part because the business went public, adding an extra layer of costs.

The company subsequently hiked the full-year sales guidance by around $2.5 million to a midpoint of $45.5 million, implying that fourth-quarter sales are seen around $12.6 million, with revenues trending up nicely on a sequential basis.

After initially falling to the low teens, shares now trade at $15 again, for a $300 million equity valuation based on a share tally of some 20 million shares. This now includes a $122 million net cash position, for a $180 million operating asset valuation, which, based on the full-year sales guidance, implies a 4 times sales multiple awarded to the business. Growth seems a bit more stable than was the case earlier this year, yet losses remain real, yet very manageable given the cash holdings operated with here.

With management talking about higher selling prices and continued growth (also in the number of indications), I expect growth to continue next year, which hopefully results in some operating leverage as well.

And Now?

Frankly, Shoulder Innovations has been performing in line with expectations since the business went public, having delivered on continued sales growth after initially guiding cautiously. A temporary increase in operating losses is not too uncommon for businesses that recently have gone public.

In between all this, I am quite cautious and impressed with the business, as shares have been trading stagnant, with the resulting operating asset valuation being very modest, while real top line momentum is seen.

This makes shares actually a prime takeover candidate in my view for larger players that have more distribution power. Going at it alone, the organic growth seems pretty reasonable enough with sufficient cash balances maintained to fund current losses.

I am pleased with the progress made so far, yet recognizing that the company has an above-average risk and return profile, I am sticking with a cautious yet interested view. For now, I am looking to update the thesis upon the release of the fourth quarter results and issuance of the 2026 guidance into the new year.

data.title: DSV: The Schenker Synergy Story
data.description:
Introduction

DSV (DSDVF) is up 19.54% YTD, and has seen some good price momentum the past weeks. The momentum started to shift back up again after DSV recent Q3 release. It seems like investors are gaining more confidence towards the Schenker integration and the synergies that come with this acquisition. In this article, I will go through DSV recent financial performance, growth outlook, valuation and risk, and explain why I believe DSV is a buy.

What has happened?

DSV financial performance has been largely impacted by DSV's acquisition of Schenker. Looking beyond the acquisition impacting the numbers, organic growth has been largely flat QoQ and YoY. The quarter was impacted by global macroeconomics uncertainties, and trade tariffs impact on flows become more visible during Q3 2025. "Volume developments showed increased volatility during the quarter, with downtrading in certain sectors, especially impacting sea freight". Despite the heavy volatile microenvironment for global trade, DSV delivered stable and results, which in itself is an achievement.

Air & Sea had some good growth, up 7.2% YoY the first nine months. Gross profit is only up 6.3% due to gross margin decrease not including Schenker numbers. Including Schenker numbers, Air & Sea saw a slight improvement in gross margin YoY the first nine months.

Road saw flat growth the last nine months. Gross profit is down due to lower gross margins, which impacted EBIT the last nine months. However, if we include the Schenker numbers, it tells a different story. Gross margin increased by 1.45% YoY, seeing the impact of Schenker mix in DSV numbers.

Contract logistics saw most growth the past quarter, up 20.7%. The last nine months have been behind, only up 3.2%. The large jump came from new contracts, customers are attracted by DSV's structure that deliveries end-to-end service.

If we include Schenker numbers, then we can see a huge increase in gross margin YoY, up 5.4%. Now gross margin stands at 44.14% the first nine months including Schenker numbers.

It is fair to say that Schenker has had a positive impact on gross margins for all segments, especially contract logistics. However, the high staff costs and other external costs from the acquisition are weighing down EBIT margin for the quarter. I will come back to DSV's integration of Schenker in the growth section.

FY 2025 outlook has changed slightly over the last quarter. Mostly positive guidance improvements, such as stronger upside scenario for EBIT. Bigger financial synergies impact from Schenker integration.

  • EBIT before special items has been narrowed to the range of DKK 19.5-20.5 billion (previously DKK 19.5-21.5 billion).
  • Full-year 2025 financial impact from synergies related to the Schenker integration now expected of around DKK 800 million (previously DKK 500-600 million).
  • Amortisation of purchase price allocations below DKK 500 million (unchanged).
  • Special items related to transaction and integration costs in the range of DKK 2.5-3.0 billion (previously DKK 2.0-2.5 billion).

Growth

Schenker integration is the biggest catalyst for growth in the upcoming years. DSV has updated shareholders during Q3 that their main focus going forward is to “Customer dialogue remains a top priority and see consistent high customer satisfaction scores and very limited customer attrition”. They have finished setting up their organisational structure, which is important. They have cut more than 3,000 white-collar employees since the integration started, and the consolidation of back-office functions will continue in Q4, 2025.

Total transaction and integration costs are still anticipated at around DKK 11.0 billion. These cost will be charged to the P&L as special items. The majority of the costs are expected in 2026 and 2027.

DSV has made a timeframe for when they believe the integration is fully complete and when they will realize most annual synergies. The impact will be 8 billion DKK by the end of 2028. If these synergies were to impact this year, EBIT margin would stand at 12.27% TTM, currently it is 8.14%. The question is: How smoothly the integration will actually plan out, and will they manage to achieve these synergies over time?

If we look at past acquisitions and promises, then it is clear that DSV has delivered on their past acquisitions. So, their past execution makes their Schenker promise more trustworthy. Panalpina and Agililty GIL were two successful acquisitions.

Low oil prices are a catalyst for DSV. Most of DSV's operations are dependent on fuel prices, and if oil prices continue to have downward pressure, that will ultimately pressure fuel prices. This could be a positive catalyst for short - near term.

Valuation

I generally use a discounted EPS valuation to determine DSV's potential valuation. However, since DSV's profitability and revenue stream is quite volatile, I will, therefore, use an EPS scenario to determine what DSV's valuation could potentially be. The EPS scenario is based on several assumptions.

There are three assumptions, and all the estimates used in the EPS scenario is from DSV guidance.

  • Uses a 5Y average PE ratio of 25x
  • Revenue growth of 5%
  • Net margin 5Y average 6.41%

I also made a sensitivity analysis, where the valuation changes based on PE ratios and net margin. If DSV's Schenker integration works out as they have planned, and they achieve stronger operating leverage combined with steady revenue growth, then there is good reason to believe that DSV could a share price beyond 2000 DKK or $313.

Risks

The risks are clear at this point. How efficient will DSV manages to execute like they have guided is important for the price to keep going. If the market sees any weakness, it will detect it fairly quickly and send DSV into another bearish regime.

Financial health has weakened over the past year. It is still strong, but the debt has increased due to the acquisition, DSV has leveraged up substantially the past year. It has now more than doubled since 2024, but I don't view it as a major concern due to high ICR of 9x, and DSV expects higher profitability in the future from synergies.

Conclusion

DSV outlook looks promising, and it is obvious that the market seems optimistic about DSV's Schenker integration, and the promised synergies they will achieve throughout the following years. If DSV manages to achieve these synergies and execute on time or ahead of it, then I believe DSV has a bright future in front of them. The main risk is that DSV has to deliver according to plan, and any diversion could change DSV trend. However, I believe DSV opportunities weigh out the potential risk, and therefore, I rate DSV a buy!

data.title: Macquarie Real Estate Securities Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

US real estate investment trusts (REITs) returned 4.8% for 3Q25. Long bond yields stayed above 4%, with persistent inflation leading to question marks around the pace of US Federal Reserve (Fed) interest rate cuts and the likely terminal yield (level where cuts stop). That said, the Fed cut rates again in September after a long pause from the previous cut in December 2024. The monetary easing was supportive of risk markets. The US Treasury yield curve continues to steepen, led by the short end, which closely follows changes to Fed policy. Broader economic statistics remained mixed, with manufacturing purchasing managers’ indices (PMIs) below trend and services PMIs above trend. Elsewhere, the labor market saw modest employment growth offset by material reductions to prior-period monthly jobs numbers, which raised questions as to the strength of the US economy.

The main source of incremental news in September came out of several REIT conferences, where most companies gave interperiod updates on operating trends since their prior quarterly reports. The standout was Americold, which put out an update that showed that operating trends continued to deteriorate, as illustrated by falling cold storage utilization levels. The stock reacted negatively and continued to underperform through the month, declining 13%. Elsewhere, it was interesting to observe the shift in sentiment toward office, with clear enthusiasm for New York prime grade office and a nascent recovery in San Francisco.

With mortgage rates beginning to decline from their peak, there is a narrative forming that single family home REITs will face more competition from the “to buy” market, as lower mortgage costs will make home ownership accessible to a broader segment of the market, many of whom have been forced to rent in recent years. Cyclical concerns aside, the single family rental subsector remained a compelling niche in the real estate market, with low portfolio churn, strong earnings growth, and material asset backing.

Conference season confirmed that the fundamental backdrop for the healthcare space remained robust, with strong occupancy, pricing power, and margin expansion driving returns. A dearth of new construction should lead to a favorable supply/demand environment, which should be supportive for the sector at the margin, particularly as demographics continue to accelerate in their key 80-plus customer cohort.

On the external growth front, feedback was also positive, with all the main players pointing to a conducive deal-making environment. The REITs have the best cost of capital, private funds are coming to the end of their investment periods and will be looking for monetizations, and assets are trading at a discount to replacement cost. With the current positive spreads versus cost of capital, incremental deals should add to earnings growth across the space.

Source: Bloomberg, unless otherwise noted.

Within the Fund

For 3Q25, Macquarie Real Estate Securities Fund Institutional Class shares posted a positive return and outperformed the Fund’s benchmark, the FTSE Nareit Equity REITs Index.

Among key contributors to the Fund’s relative performance, an overweight to Welltower Inc. (WELL) contributed to performance as the sector continued to face good structural tailwinds with aging US demographics supporting demand for senior housing facilities. At the same time, supply of these facilities has been restricted due to unfeasible construction economics at current levels. Therefore, companies like Welltower, with in-place portfolios, have benefited from the supply-and-demand imbalance.

An overweight allocation to NEXTDC Ltd. (NXDCF), an Australian data center operator, also contributed to Fund returns. NEXTDC’s stock price jumped after a strong quarterly earnings report in late August. Its stock trades off the forward order book given the amount of potential capacity in the business and the weight of tenant demand. Accordingly, positive news on progress around signing incremental tenants was well received by the market.

Among key detractors, American Tower Corp. (AMT) was affected by rate sensitivity, slugging Latin American growth, and foreign currency losses that have dampened its organic momentum. We continue to see long-term value in American Tower along with strong fundamental structural tailwinds supporting the sector. An overweight allocation to Invitation Homes Inc. (INVH) detracted from relative returns. The stock price declined as higher interest rates weighed on investor sentiment, making it pricier for landlords to borrow and a drag on real estate values. We continue to see value in the name.

Outlook

Looking forward, the global REIT market is underpinned by expectations of further interest rate cuts and selective earnings growth across regions. Although higher rates remain a challenge, the prospect of easing monetary policy continues to support investor sentiment. Sectors driven by long-term structural trends – such as data centers and healthcare – are particularly well-placed to benefit from ongoing global demand. A disciplined investment strategy should prioritize high-quality assets that offer reliable income, while targeted allocations to undervalued or cyclical sectors may present attractive opportunities as markets respond to shifting economic conditions.
