data.title: Abivax: Here's Why Eli Lilly Could Want Its Oral Ulcerative Colitis Therapy
data.description:
Thesis

There has been a lot of positivity surrounding ABIVAX (ABVX) stock in 2025. Just recently, it was named as the best performing biotech stock year to date with a massive 1,470% run-up. The company has also been progressing their lead candidate Obefazimod through the pipeline, which has served as a big catalyst for the stock, hitting primary targets. Now, over the past few weeks, we've had another big stock-moving bit of news in that Eli Lilly (LLY) has some kind of interest in taking over Abivax. Now, I see that Lilly's interest is genuine, as Obefazimod would fit quite nicely into their existing offerings, as I'll explain. Obefazimod is also an oral drug, which adds a lot of attractiveness in terms of saleability. However, I would advise investors to price Abivax's future movements on the success of Obefazimod alone and not expect an inminat take over from Lilly. Since a good amount of stock movement has been priced in from the takeover speculation, if we see no such takeover, I would expect a sharp pullback of the stock, which is the main risk for buying at this stage. Which is why I am only hold-rated on the stock. I see upside potential to this stock, but the size of the run-up so far is just too much.

3Q25

Abivax released its 3Q25 financials not too long ago, and we're starting to see the company go into an advanced investment phase. We saw them significantly strengthening their balance sheet whilst still handling materially higher operating and non-cash expenses. The expenses were, of course driven by their late-stage clinical development. For the nine months ended September 30th, the operating loss increased quite a bit to €174.4 million, up €44.2 million year over year, which was mainly from a substantial increase in R&D spending, which hit €133.4 million. As you know, Abivax is busy advancing its Phase 3 ulcerative colitis program and ramping up clinical activity in Crohn's disease and additional Obefazimod studies. General and administrative expenses also jumped quite sharply to €41.8 million, which is largely a reflection of share-price-driven employer social charges, which are tied to equity awards. There was also the added cost of operating as a dual-listed public company. On the other hand, sales and marketing expenses actually declined since some prior-year rebranding costs rolled off.

Net loss for the period hit a hefty €254.1 million, which was mostly influenced by non-cash items, including €65.9 million related to fair-value increases of convertible notes and warrants following share price appreciation. This was €22.5 million in share-based compensation and foreign exchange revaluation effects, which goes to show that headline losses were not entirely cash-driven. Crucially, however, Abivax exited the quarter with a pretty strong liquidity position. They now hold about €589.7 million in cash and cash equivalents, a figure which is up from €144.2 million at year-end 2024. This follows their July 2025 U.S. public offering, which generated about €597 million in net proceeds, and managed to reduce debt quite a bit via conversions and planned repayments. Management has mentioned that their cash balance should support a funding runway into the fourth quarter of 2027. Total cash burn over the last 9 months was €137.9 million used in operations, which puts the average monthly cash burn in the €15.3 million per month range or an annualised cash burn run-rate of €183.6 million per year.

The pipeline

Now, Abivax's pipeline is pretty focused on its lead candidate, Obefazimod (ABX464). It's an oral small-molecule designed to enhance miR-124 and reduce inflammation. Obefazimod, as I'll explain shortly, is currently in pivotal Phase 3 development for moderately to severely active ulcerative colitis as part of the global ABTECT program. The program has already completed enrollment and reported positive 8-week induction results in 2025. It's now advancing the 44-week maintenance phase with data expected sometime in mid-2026. If we see maintenance data as supportive, we can expect them to move into regulatory submissions later in 2026, which makes this the most promising and closest to potential approval.

Elsewhere, Abivax is also advancing Obefazimod in Phase 2b for Crohn's disease, with patient enrollment completion and initial data expected in 2H26. Now, beyond just Obefazimod, Abivax has exploratory research into some combination regimens with other agents in inflammatory bowel disease. They're currently working on follow-on compounds, which might be able to leverage their miR-124 mechanism, but these are only in preclinical/earlier research stages and far from any kind of regulatory submission.

Obefazimod

So Obefazimod is their lead drug candidate, and it is also a first-in-class oral small molecule which is designed to treat moderately to severely active ulcerative colitis (UC). Now, the compound is actually designed to modulate the immune system rather than just broadly suppressing it. Mechanistically speaking, it does this by binding to the cap-binding complex, which is involved in RNA biogenesis. Now this binding is what selectively enhances the splicing of a long non-coding RNA to produce the anti-inflammatory microRNA miR-124. Elevated miR-124 levels are what downregulate some very important pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines, such as TNF-α, IL-6, IL-17 and MCP-1, and also reduce pathogenic Th17 cells. This is effectively what I would describe as a physiological brake on inflammation without blunting normal immune function elsewhere.

Clinically, the compound has shown us some pretty positive results across multiple studies. Back in Phase 2a and Phase 2b trials, a once-daily oral dosing met primary endpoints, which included quite significant reductions in disease activity scores and also improvements in clinical remission, endoscopic improvement, and biomarkers compared with placebo. It also demonstrated a sustained benefit and very favourable tolerability over long-term maintenance follow-up. Most importantly, however, in the global Phase 3 ABTECT induction program, Obefazimod managed to meet the FDA-defined primary endpoint of clinical remission at Week 8 at a 50 mg daily dose in both pivotal trials (ABTECT-1 and ABTECT-2). I see this as a big de-risking aspect to the program. It should set Obefazimod up quite nicely to advance toward Phase 3 maintenance data and a potential future regulatory submission if durability and safety are confirmed. The Phase 3 ABTECT program also saw Obefazimod post statistically significant placebo-adjusted remission rates and highly significant secondary efficacy outcomes. There were also no new safety signals. I put together the table below to summarise the progress made with Obefazimod so far.

A word on the TAM

Now, the TAM for Obefazimod, which is being developed mainly for moderately to severely active UC, which is what I'll focus on here, is very significant and growing. The global UC drug market has a current value in the high single-digit to low double-digit billions of USD annually. Some estimates place the value of the market at around $7.6 to $10.5 billion in 2025, with projections rising toward the $12 to $14 billion mark by the early 2030s. Unfortunately, prevalence is increasing, and the adoption of advanced therapies is expanding. Now, within this market, advanced biologics and targeted small molecules would account for the largest share, particularly those used in moderate-to-severe disease types where we can expect Obefazimod to be positioned.

Just to touch quickly on the competition, I see Obefazimod competing with some pretty established biologics like vedolizumab (Entyvio), ustekinumab (Stelara), and a few anti-TNF agents such as Humira/adalimumab, Remicade/infliximab. There are also quite a few JAK inhibitors on the market, such as upadacitinib (Rinvoq). Now, these existing therapies are often chronic, very high-cost treatments. We typically see annual biologic therapy costs in the range of tens of thousands of dollars per patient per year in the U.S. There are also some regimens exceeding $25,000 to $30,000 annually, depending on the drug and dosing, whilst oral JAK inhibitors like Rinvoq can be quite expensive with list prices around $6,700 per month before discounts.

Outsider interest, what could Eli Lily want

As you probably know, there is growing speculation that Lilly has a lot of interest in Abivax. I also see this interest as being quite strategically coherent when you take a look, in detail, at Lilly's focus on immunology, inflammation, and differentiated oral therapies. I think Lilly is looking to somehow complement their biologics-heavy portfolio and is currently exploring options. We know that Lilly already has a pretty strong presence in immune-mediated diseases. They have assets like mirikizumab/IL-23, which is already approved for ulcerative colitis and olumiant, a JAK inhibitor. However, both of these therapies are facing a lot of competition and payer pressure. There are also some concerns about the long-term safety scrutiny.

So in that sense, Obefazimod would give them a first-in-class, oral, non-immunosuppressive mechanism that is highly differentiated from any kind of cytokine blockade and JAK inhibition. This could set Lilly up with a much broader and more resilient UC franchise across different lines of therapy. Big pharma, obviously, sees drugs in terms of market sizes, and a way they like to take market is actually by removing competition. So by having Obefazimod, it could be one less competitor against them, and their own portfolio would then consist of some quite differentiated drugs. Another big thing we have to mention is that Obefazimod is oral and is tipped to be taken just once a day. As you know, oral drugs are far more popular among the public. Most other advanced therapies on the market are injectable biologics. So vedolizumab (Entyvio, IV or SC), ustekinumab (Stelara, IV induction then SC), and mirikizumab (IL-23 inhibitor, SC) are all some kind of injectable. Its a massive differentiator for Obefazimod.

Just to touch briefly on the mechanistic side of things, I also see Obefazimod's miR-124-mediated immune modulation as being complementary to Lilly's current biologics rather than cannibalistic. It would, of course, enable lifecycle management strategies such as sequencing or combination use later down the line. From a pipeline perspective, acquiring Abivax would also give Lilly some optionality beyond just ulcerative colitis, since Obefazimod also may have applicability to Crohn's disease and potentially some other chronic inflammatory indications. This would extend peak-sales durability for Lilly. Finally, Abivax is already Phase 3-de-risked and pretty well funded, as I mentioned. It wouldn't be the biggest gamble we've seen, Lilly. They would essentially be getting a near-commercial asset that could well scale quite rapidly within its global infrastructure. So I see Abivax as more of a strategically attractive bolt-on rather than a speculative early-stage bet.

The risk of buying in now

Abivax is currently trading with a market cap of about $9.1 billion and an enterprise value of $8.5 billion. Now, despite minimal revenue, which is giving us extremely high EV to revenue multiples (1,320x to 1,365× forward and trailing). If you glance over the valuation page, you can see the high multiples I'm talking about. So the company's valuation would be mostly driven by the potential of Obefazimod, rather than by earnings or sales. They also hold cash reserves of $692 million and near-term debt of just $87 million. So I see investors as effectively paying for pipeline potential, de-risked clinical data, and the possibility of a near-term regulatory submission to come as the next big stock mover. So the price run-up in recent times would be dependent on that rather than traditional revenue-based metrics.

On the 6-month chart, the stock is up a whopping 2170%, and over the past 5 days, the stock has jumped over 25%. That 5-day run-up has mainly been fueled by the Lilly takeover speculation. Now, because of the massive run-up, I would be cautious to buy in heavily at this stage. It seems to me that the acquisition has been priced in somewhat, albeit not fully. It means that if this does remain as just speculation, we can expect a sharp pullback in stock price, which is my worry. Lilly may well just be exploring their options and have other interests elsewhere.

data.title: Applied Materials: Memory Market Boom, China Fears, And Awaiting FY26 Outlook
data.description:
Applied Materials (NASDAQ:AMAT) is one of the key suppliers of semiconductor manufacturing equipment, providing manufacturing equipment like deposition and etching machines that are key in the AI boom today. As I mentioned in my last AMAT article, I believe one of the problem AMAT continues to face is the fact that it has a huge exposure to China, as well as being more of a jack of all trades whilst compared to many of the existing semiconductor manufacturing equipment providers, as many of them have their own niche like Lam (molybdenum deposition) and ASML (EUV lithography). Due to this factor, AMAT's revenue fell YoY in the most recent quarter when growth rates across the board have been affected especially with the China impact, which is not a good look when we are in the AI boom and most of its peers grew double digits YoY in the most recent quarter. AMAT trades at a discount to its peers, but I don't believe the discount justifies a buy just yet from its current fundamentals. With a solid quarter from Micron (MU) and demand soaring, with storage prices rising with supply constraints, AMAT still has a catalyst looking forward despite the huge China constraint. Hence, I'm maintaining AMAT at a 'Hold'.

HBM Supply Constrained

This quarter overall continued to show signs that supply in the memory market continues to get constrained, which is resulting in price increases. With constrained demand, it is likely that suppliers will continue to purchase more equipment, which essentially sets up for a good 2H of 2026 with this catalyst ahead. I believe this is also one of the main reasons that since my last AMAT article, even though they posted negative revenue growth YoY as the market realised and priced in the major catalyst.

From the UBS Global Technology and AI Conference at the start of December:

When we look in the second half, we've been working with our customers for the last quarter on getting more and more visibility into the road map for the next couple of years. And the reason is we started hearing discussions about more capacity being put in place,

But the strength and the growth that we expect will be in the leading-edge in DRAM.

This essentially highlights the strength in DRAM for 2026, as it was also reported that South Korean tech companies are raising prices on their DRAM and NAND by up to 30% in Q4, as the AI infrastructure buildout continues, which continues to boost demand for AMAT. The most recent Micron earnings also solidify this, with reports stating it increased its prices up to 20% QoQ with its HBM supply sold out through CY26. With their road maps likely to include more and more capex towards fabs, AMAT is essentially in a prime position to take advantage in FY26.

AMAT guided for $6.85B in revenue in Q1 FY26, which suggests a -4.5% YoY growth, affected by their huge proportion of revenue in China, which is a negative sign regarding recent events. The full FY26 guidance is unknown, but the next 2 quarters are important to monitor AMAT in how well they do as revenue will be more weighted towards 2H FY26, as Q1 is expecting negative growth, which is rather bearish for an AI related company, though it is transitioning away from China as China remains a huge proportion of its revenue.

AMAT and China

As explained in my Lam article, the affiliate rule creates huge restrictions on exports towards entities that are on the entity list, halting AMAT's ability to ship certain manufacturing equipment to China. China themselves also trying to improve its domestic prospects, from the earnings call:

The biggest change we've seen in our competitive position in the near term is trade restrictions. We used to serve the entire global WFE market before restrictions were implemented. By '24, 2024, we were restricted from serving around 10% of China's WFE market, mainly in leading-edge logic and the domestic NAND market. And then in the last month of 2024 and the first month of 2025, the restrictions significantly increased for us, and we could no longer serve China's DRAM market and some of the ICAPS market. So our impact grew to well over 20% of the China WFE market, which I think everybody knows that the WFE market in China has been elevated this year and also over the past few years with China approaching 40% of total WFE

AMAT doesn't benefit from the trade war from either side, as China seeks to improve its manufacturing equipment potential, whilst the US places restrictions on AMAT on what machines it can ship to China. It essentially gets affected from both sides and an escalated trade war like the start of the year has worsened things, as we continue to see more and more escalations today, which is still affecting AMAT (affiliate rule was in October). It also came out recently that China is deliberating a package of incentives and subsidies for its own chipmaking industry, shifting away from taking whatever the US allows tech firms to ship over with the huge restrictions. This would affect AMAT quite hard as mentioned before, they are not specialised in one specific machine but rather offer machines across the board, which includes ICAPS (IoT, communications, auto, power sensors) which are focused on more mature process technologies, as those are more likely to be caught up first.

Even though their proportion in China has fallen from 37% in FY24 to 30% in FY25, that remains a huge chunk of revenue that is in China. It was also noted that it will cost them $600M in lost revenue in FY26, as the amount of lost revenue could easily worsen if there are further escalations. This remains one of the biggest constraints for AMAT, so despite a catalyst incoming with HBM clearly being a supply constraint in 2026, look out for 2 things: guidance for the rest of the year and the development of China and the US regarding exporting semiconductor manufacturing equipment.

Valuation

AMAT is trading higher than its historical averages and the sector median on most aspects, and deservingly so for being one of the key players in the AI boom. AMAT deserves a premium, but the question now is how much of a premium it deserves. Analyst reports also mentioned that AMAT holds a valuation edge amongst its closest peers, comparing AMAT with its peers now:

Excluding compared to TSMC's P/E, AMAT's P/E and P/S are trading at a significant discount compared to peers like Lam Research, KLAC and ASML, with P/E trading with an upside of around 30% to its peers.

The main question for investors now is whether AMAT can actually bounce back in 2H, as its current level is rather speculative with the China tension clearly having a huge impact on AMAT. The most recent quarter had negative growth, with negative growth is also expected in the next quarter. Growth rates have been stunted across the board, but AMAT's growth prospects are way lower compared to its peers. If AMAT bounces back, the upside could easily outperform its peers considering its current valuations, but the next quarters are very important for AMAT to highlight what will happen in the near term. Compared to AMAT's peers, AMAT is trading at a discount and its current levels do look attractive compared to AMAT's peers, but remember that it is already trading higher than the sector median and historical averages.

Final Thoughts

AMAT is in a precarious situation where the memory market is facing supply constraints, which in theory should lead to soaring demand for AMAT being one of the key suppliers, but with China tensions and roadmap seemingly set up for the 2nd half of 2026, as mentioned in their conferences and earnings calls, AMAT short-term prospects has to be closely monitored as further development and escalations could easily impact AMAT in the short term. If the tension eases in the near term with the memory market continuing to boom as it is, AMAT's growth rate could easily impress in the 2H of 2026, but for now I want more clarity on China as well as the guidance for 2H of FY26.

data.title: Top 5 Viral Humanoid Moments That Made 2025 The Year Of Robotics
data.description:
Seemingly overnight, humanoids are crowding social media feeds and headlining major financial news outlets. Five viral moments in particular, driven by breakthroughs from Tesla, 1X, XPeng, Figure AI, and UBTECH, have not only captured global attention but redefined the trajectory of the industry itself. Now, the same companies are racing to turn viral moments into results, scaling mass production and staking their claim in what could become a $5 trillion industry.1

KraneShares sparked our own wave of media buzz earlier this year when we had a Unitree G1 Ultimate humanoid robot, nicknamed “KOID-bot,” ring the Nasdaq opening bell to celebrate the launch of the KraneShares Global Humanoid & Embodied Intelligence Index ETF (KOID). KOID-bot trended on social media platforms like TikTok and Instagram, appeared on live segments from FOX Business, Yahoo Finance, and Cheddar, and was featured in print in Barron's, the New York Post, and The Times in the UK.

The KOID ETF is designed to capture the broad opportunity in the robotics and embodied intelligence ecosystem. KOID’s portfolio focuses on three key categories: the “brain” (semiconductors & technology), the “body” (actuation systems, mechanical systems, sensing & perception, and critical materials), and humanoid “integrators” (manufacturing companies).

In this article, we will cover five “integrators” beyond Unitree that we believe have a first-mover advantage towards mass production and market adoption.

Tesla’s Optimus Gen 3

Tesla (TSLA) has ushered in multiple viral moments showcasing its Optimus Gen 3 humanoid. Perhaps its most impressive public appearance was in a Tesla diner. An all-black Optimus Gen 3 showcased lifelike movement and dexterity as it served popcorn to customers, adding fuel to the humanoid robotics media frenzy.

@teslaownerssv Optimus serving popcorn at the Tesla Diner #fyp #viral #tesla #optimus #teslarobot ♬ original sound - Tesla Owners Silicon Valley

Plans for Tesla’s latest humanoid were highlighted during the company’s 2025 shareholder meeting and have created widespread excitement among both investors and consumers. According to Tesla’s website, the company wants to “create a general-purpose, bi-pedal, autonomous humanoid robot capable of performing unsafe, repetitive, or boring tasks.” We believe this aligns with long-term industry goals and provides a clearer path to easy adoption compared to a robot designed for a specific use case.

Tesla stated that initial deployments will begin with internal use at Tesla factories before becoming available to the public. Tesla currently has a “pilot production line” (an early, small-scale manufacturing setup) active at its Fremont, California factory. This means the company is already assembling early versions (“pilots”) of the Optimus robots to test procedures and refine the design before mass production.

Updates on Tesla’s official X account indicate that the company is targeting a total cost of goods sold (COGS) of $20,000 per robot “at scale” (meaning once they reach high-volume manufacturing), which is the expense required to manufacture each robot.2 We believe that lowering the COGS will be crucial for achieving profitability and affordability for customers. Like Apple's iPhone, Tesla is also planning a rapid annual upgrade cycle: Gen 4 is expected in 2027, and Gen 5 in 2028.2

Tesla's competitive edge lies in its vertically integrated supply chain and substantial factory network. Nearly all core components, electronics, and actuators are designed in-house, including overlap advantages with its electric vehicle business, which we believe will help Tesla successfully implement aggressive cost-down strategies. In addition to ramping up production lines in its Fremont factory, Tesla is also expected to utilize its Gigafactory Texas for Optimus production. Elon Musk stated at Tesla’s 2025 shareholder meeting that the “Optimus' pilot line will be capable of producing up to one million units annually... and this would just be the beginning. In the future ... production could literally be out of this world.”

Tesla's unique scale, margin potential, and industry know-how make it a potential frontrunner in the robotics space. Tesla has a 2.08% weight in KOID as of 10/31/2025.3

1X’s Neo Gamma

In October 2025, Norwegian robotics company 1X stunned consumers with the unveiling of its Neo Gamma robot. In 1X’s viral Neo robot video, the Neo robot featured unique capabilities, such as scheduling and executing tasks like cleaning, doing laundry, answering the door, turning off lights, and organizing spaces with simple app or voice commands.

TikTok - Make Your Day NEO Gamma in homes near you #fyp #robotics #tech #ai #humanoid #technology ♬original sound - 1X

Neo is designed as an all-in-one home assistant robot that can seamlessly integrate into daily routines. 1X CEO Bernt Børnich said, “With NEO Gamma, every engineering and design decision was made with one goal in mind: getting NEO into customers’ homes as quickly as possible… We’re close. We can’t wait to share more soon.” Priced at $20,000 for complete ownership (or consumers can opt for the $499 monthly subscription fee), Neo is available for pre-order now, with first deliveries expected in Q2 2026.​​4

For any task Neo doesn’t yet understand, owners will be able to book a remote session with a 1X expert who can teach the robot this new chore. Børnich stated, “There is a not-so-distant future where we all have our own robot helper at home, like Rosey the Robot or Baymax. But for humanoid robots to truly integrate into everyday life, they must be developed alongside humans, not in isolation.” This workflow would enable the Neo robot to learn continuously from both human experts and real-world use, even after being sold, and for Neo to be tailored to individual customer needs.

Neo stands 5'6" tall, weighs 66 pounds, and features a soft 3D lattice polymer body for safe movement and easy machine washing, making it comfortable and practical for home environments.4 Its advanced Redwood AI vision-language model powers autonomous navigation, human gesture understanding, and personalized memory, empowering Neo to recommend recipes from fridge contents, converse naturally, and even provide reminders for misplaced keys or medication schedules.​​

As the market for home robotics heats up, we believe Neo represents a watershed moment: an affordable, consumer-ready helper that learns, adapts, and improves with every software update.

XPeng’s Next-Gen IRON

XPeng’s (XPEV) Next-Gen IRON humanoid robot stole the show at XPENG AI Day, which also featured plans for flying cars and robotaxis in Guangzhou, China, on November 5, 2025. Consumers were impressed not just because of its technical breakthroughs, but because they thought it was a real human in a costume. When IRON walked on stage, its movements were so natural and lifelike that social media lit up with rumors and disbelief. People speculated that this was a publicity stunt or a “fake,” convinced that IRON’s body language and mannerisms couldn’t possibly be a robot.​​

XPeng leaned into the excitement and addressed the skepticism head-on: the company’s CEO performed a “robot reveal” in front of a live audience, opening IRON’s artificial skin and exposing its metal frame, wiring, and actuators right on stage while the robot continued to operate. This dramatic moment, caught on video and widely shared, definitively proved IRON was not a human in disguise but a breakthrough in what engineers call “extreme anthropomorphism.” In plain terms: IRON is built with a spine-like structural core, muscle-mimicking actuators, and soft skin designed to look and flex like real flesh. The hands themselves have 22 degrees of movement, allowing IRON to gesture, wave, and interact fluidly with people, mirroring human motion so closely that even experts were initially fooled.​​5

@chinaminutes China’s XPeng recently revealed a humanoid robot named IRON, drawing global attention after the CEO cut open its leg on stage to prove it wasn’t a human in disguise. Designed with a bionic spine, 82 joints and ultra-realistic “skin,” IRON’s gait and movement were so lifelike that audiences speculated a human was inside. IRON is powered by three AI chips capable of processing over 2,000 trillion operations per second. It can walk, balance, and make gestures that closely resemble human movement. The company says IRON is built for real-world roles, from corporate assistance to retail and possibly domestic settings, and is aimed for commercial rollout by late 2026. The company demonstrated IRON during its AI Day event in Guangzhou, presenting it as part of China’s growing push into humanoid robotics.... #humanoid #chinatech #airevolution #xpeng #robot #son original - Trendformusic

XPeng’s goal for IRON isn’t to replace people but to make robots feel comfortable, trustworthy, and approachable in public spaces like stores, museums, and offices. The company is starting IRON’s real-world journey in these human-facing jobs, including shop guides, receptionists, and tour assistants. Mass production is planned for 2026, and as the technology improves and costs fall, we believe IRON will become even more accessible.5 This media sensation has now positioned IRON as one of the world’s most talked-about robotics innovations, demonstrating that the future of “physical AI” isn’t just about advanced chips and engineering, but about making robots feel at home among us.​​5

We believe XPeng’s IRON represents both a leap in what robots can physically do and a cultural milestone in how society will accept them, thanks to a debut that truly blurred the line between human and machine. XPeng has a 1.97% weight in KOID as of 10/31/2025.3

Figure AI’s Figure 03

Figure AI’s Figure 03 made a splash in October 2025 with a headline-grabbing debut, following months of anticipation in the robotics world. Figure's viral moment was similar to 1X's, as videos of its autonomous robot showcased skills like organizing a messy living room and gathering dirty dishes to clean in the kitchen. On Figure AI's official YouTube page, they racked up over 5.5 million views.

@xrobohub Watch it work! Figure 03 doing long-horizon tasks FULLY autonomously at home! #HumanoidRobot #FigureAI #robotics ♬ 原声 - XRoboHub

Figure AI’s website plainly states the company’s mission: “Our goal is to deliver a truly general-purpose robot - one that can perform human-like tasks and learn directly from people.” Videos circulating online show the robot smoothly folding laundry, loading dishwashers, wheeling out trash, and chatting politely, giving consumers a strong sense that this isn’t just some “future tech” demo, but something ready for regular, everyday use.​​

The story behind Figure 03’s development is one of total reinvention. Figure AI rebuilt everything according to its company website: “To realize this vision, our engineering and design teams completed a ground-up hardware and software redesign to ship Figure 03.” Its signature Helix brain was upgraded, its vision system was improved for better navigation in messy homes or busy offices, and its hands and skin were reworked for a gentler, safer touch.6 The robot can now navigate through narrow hallways, avoid pinching hazards, and even identify slippery or delicate objects with new tactile sensors in its fingertips that can sense the weight of a paper clip.6

Figure AI’s BotQ factory, which is “Figure’s dedicated manufacturing facility designed to scale robot production,” is a “first-generation manufacturing line that will initially be capable of producing up to 12,000 humanoid robots per year, with the goal of producing a total of 100,000 robots over the next four years.”6 Additionally, Figure AI is rolling out Figure 03 in pilots across both commercial sites and residential communities, collecting real-world data to refine its performance.

Figure 03’s $80,000+ price tag initially targets deep-pocketed partners, but each new deployment draws Figure closer to serving ordinary households and transforming work as we know it.​6

UBTECH’s Walker S2

UBTECH’s (UBTRF) Walker S2 has captured global manufacturing headlines in 2025, with footage of entire robot armies, reminiscent of scenes in the movie iRobot, being prepared for shipment in China. This video was the most recent viral humanoid robotics moment, unsurprisingly garnered over 15 million views on three separate TikTok accounts.

@dailymail Chinese tech company, UBTECH Robotics, shows off their latest humanoid robots with a video that looks closer to a marching army rather than a showcase. UBTECH, located in Shenzhen, China, has an array of designs in stock, including some AI bots that take more of an animal-like form. The 'Walker S2' model is able to autonomously switch out its own battery, which takes approximately 3 minutes to complete, and it can walk around at a top speed of 4.5 mph. UBTECH Robotics #china #tech #robot #science #news ♬ original sound - Daily Mail

The focus here is all business: Walker S2 isn’t being pitched as a home companion; it’s being handed straight to the world’s busiest factories and logistics warehouses, where labor shortages and 24/7 demands are the norm. When BYD, Geely, Foxconn, and other leading firms need boots on the ground, they’re now picking Walker S2 to carry inventory, assemble parts, and keep operations running at high speed.​

The Walker S2’s strongest suit is its seamless integration into high-pressure industrial settings. When it runs out of battery life, the robot takes itself to a swap station, pops in a charged pack, and is back at work in under 5 minutes.7 We believe this process is key to maximizing output on production lines that don’t rest. UBTECH’s orders, totaling over $110 million already, underscore that this isn’t about replacing workers but making factories more resilient to staffing shortages, last-minute order surges, and late-night shifts.7

For investors looking for where “robots at work” is a business reality and not just a tech promise, few images capture the shift better than the rows of Walker S2 units lined up and ready for deployment. UBTECH has a 1.96% weight in KOID as of 10/31/2025.3

KOID ETF Performance Since Inception

Conclusion

The landscape for humanoid robotics in 2025 has matured at an unexpected pace, with global players such as Tesla, 1X, XPeng, Figure AI, and UBTECH advancing both technical capabilities and production scale. These companies are not only demonstrating breakthroughs in lifelike movement, adaptability, and autonomous reasoning but are also driving down costs and forging supply chain advantages we believe could bring millions of robots into homes, factories, and service roles within the next few years.​

For investors, the KraneShares Global Humanoid & Embodied Intelligence Index ETF offers exposure to this potential megatrend at the ground floor. KOID is the first ETF of its kind to focus on companies directly enabling humanoid robots through foundational technologies like sensors, actuators, AI chips, and advanced manufacturing, in addition to the headline robot makers themselves.8 By equally weighting holdings and investing across multiple geographies, we believe KOID provides investors with a balanced, future-forward stake in the market’s evolution while potentially lowering the risk of overconcentration in a single player or geography.​
